skills.prompt: This topic covers DNA and RNA structure for AP Biology students (CED: IST-6.A, Unit 6, Topic 6.1). Students should understand the structural differences between DNA and RNA: DNA is double-stranded with deoxyribose sugar, uses thymine, and forms a stable double helix for long-term information storage. RNA is single-stranded with ribose sugar, uses uracil, and exists in multiple forms (mRNA, tRNA, rRNA) for different functions. Nucleotides are linked by phosphodiester bonds in a 5-prime to 3-prime direction. The antiparallel orientation and complementary base pairing (A-T/U, G-C) are essential for replication and transcription. Students must compare DNA and RNA structures and explain how structure relates to function. Connect to replication, transcription, gene regulation, and biotechnology. AP exam note: diagram-based questions on nucleic acid structure are common. Vocabulary: deoxyribose, ribose, phosphodiester bond, antiparallel, complementary base pairing, double helix.

This topic covers DNA replication for AP Biology students (CED: IST-6.B, Unit 6, Topic 6.2). Students should understand that replication is semiconservative: each new DNA molecule contains one original and one new strand. Key enzymes include helicase (unwinds double helix), primase (synthesizes RNA primers), DNA polymerase III (adds nucleotides 5-prime to 3-prime, proofreads), and ligase (joins Okazaki fragments on the lagging strand). The leading strand is synthesized continuously; the lagging strand is synthesized in Okazaki fragments. Origins of replication allow bidirectional replication. Telomerase extends telomeres in germ cells and stem cells, preventing chromosome shortening. Students must describe the replication process and explain why it is semiconservative. Connect to cell division, mutations, aging, and cancer. AP exam note: Meselson-Stahl experiment evidence is important. Vocabulary: semiconservative, helicase, primase, DNA polymerase, ligase, Okazaki fragment, leading strand, lagging strand, telomere, telomerase.

This topic covers transcription and RNA processing for AP Biology students (CED: IST-6.C, Unit 6, Topic 6.3). Students should understand that transcription produces mRNA from a DNA template using RNA polymerase, which reads the template strand 3-prime to 5-prime and synthesizes mRNA 5-prime to 3-prime. In eukaryotes, pre-mRNA undergoes processing: addition of a 5-prime cap (modified guanine), a 3-prime poly-A tail, and removal of introns by spliceosomes (RNA splicing), leaving exons joined together. Alternative splicing allows one gene to produce multiple proteins. In prokaryotes, mRNA is not processed and translation can begin before transcription finishes. Students must compare prokaryotic and eukaryotic transcription and explain the significance of RNA processing. Connect to gene regulation, protein diversity, and evolution. AP exam note: questions on RNA processing steps are common. Vocabulary: RNA polymerase, template strand, coding strand, promoter, intron, exon, spliceosome, alternative splicing, 5-prime cap, poly-A tail.

This topic covers translation for AP Biology students (CED: IST-6.D, Unit 6, Topic 6.4). Students should understand that translation converts mRNA codons into an amino acid sequence (polypeptide) at ribosomes. Transfer RNA (tRNA) carries amino acids and has anticodons complementary to mRNA codons. The genetic code is universal, redundant (degenerate), and non-overlapping. Translation occurs in three stages: initiation (ribosome assembles at start codon AUG), elongation (amino acids added via peptide bonds at A, P, E sites), and termination (stop codon reached, polypeptide released). Students must use a codon chart to determine amino acid sequences and predict effects of mutations on protein structure. Connect to mutations, gene expression, and post-translational modification. AP exam note: codon chart interpretation and mutation analysis are heavily tested. Vocabulary: codon, anticodon, ribosome, tRNA, mRNA, start codon, stop codon, peptide bond, A site, P site, E site, polypeptide.

This topic covers regulation of gene expression for AP Biology students (CED: IST-6.E, Unit 6, Topic 6.5). Students should understand that gene expression is regulated at multiple levels: chromatin remodeling (epigenetic modifications like DNA methylation and histone acetylation), transcriptional regulation (transcription factors, enhancers, silencers, promoters), post-transcriptional regulation (alternative splicing, mRNA stability, miRNA), translational regulation, and post-translational modification (phosphorylation, ubiquitination). In prokaryotes, operons (lac and trp) coordinate gene regulation. In eukaryotes, regulation is more complex due to chromatin structure and long-range regulatory elements. Students must explain how differential gene expression leads to cell specialization despite identical DNA. Connect to development, cancer, and epigenetics. AP exam note: operon models and eukaryotic regulation are heavily tested. Vocabulary: operon, promoter, operator, repressor, activator, enhancer, epigenetic, methylation, histone, transcription factor.

skills.skill: IST 6.6 Gene Expression and Cell Specialization
skills.prompt: This topic covers gene expression and cell specialization for AP Biology students (CED: IST-6.F, Unit 6, Topic 6.6). Students should understand that all cells in a multicellular organism contain the same DNA, but differential gene expression determines cell type and function. During development, cells become specialized through the selective activation and silencing of genes controlled by transcription factors, signaling molecules, and epigenetic modifications. Examples include muscle cells expressing myosin genes and red blood cells expressing hemoglobin genes. Stem cells retain the ability to differentiate into multiple cell types. Induction by neighboring cells influences cell fate during embryonic development. Students must explain how gene regulation produces diverse cell types. Connect to signal transduction, development, and regenerative medicine. AP exam note: questions may present gene expression data from different tissues. Vocabulary: differentiation, cell specialization, stem cell, totipotent, pluripotent, induction, morphogen, homeotic gene.

This topic covers mutations for AP Biology students (CED: IST-6.G, Unit 6, Topic 6.7). Students should understand that mutations are changes in DNA sequence that can be spontaneous or induced by mutagens (UV light, chemicals, radiation). Types include point mutations: substitutions (silent, missense, nonsense) and insertions/deletions (causing frameshifts). Chromosomal mutations include large-scale deletions, duplications, inversions, and translocations. Some mutations are beneficial (providing variation for natural selection), some are neutral (due to redundancy in the genetic code), and some are harmful (causing genetic disorders or cancer). Students must predict the effect of specific mutations on protein structure and function using the codon chart. Connect to evolution, cancer, genetic disorders, and gene regulation. AP exam note: mutation analysis with codon charts is very common. Vocabulary: point mutation, substitution, insertion, deletion, frameshift, silent, missense, nonsense, mutagen, carcinogen.

This topic covers biotechnology for AP Biology students (CED: IST-6.H, Unit 6, Topic 6.8). Students should understand key biotechnology techniques: PCR (polymerase chain reaction) amplifies specific DNA segments using primers, Taq polymerase, and thermal cycling. Gel electrophoresis separates DNA fragments by size (smaller fragments migrate farther). Restriction enzymes cut DNA at specific recognition sequences, enabling recombinant DNA technology. Bacterial transformation introduces foreign DNA via plasmid vectors. DNA sequencing determines nucleotide order. CRISPR-Cas9 allows precise genome editing. Applications include genetic engineering, gene therapy, forensics, and GMO production. Students must describe techniques and analyze gel electrophoresis results. Connect to gene expression, cloning, and ethical considerations. AP exam note: interpreting gel electrophoresis and experimental design with biotechnology tools are common. Vocabulary: PCR, gel electrophoresis, restriction enzyme, plasmid, recombinant DNA, transformation, CRISPR, GMO, DNA fingerprinting.
